stories.headline: Denver police shoot armed suspect after foot chase on 16th Street following earlier downtown shooting
stories.summary:
Two Denver Police Department officers shot an armed man in downtown Denver early Sunday morning after he refused to drop a weapon during a foot chase near 16th Street. The suspect, who was wanted in connection with an earlier shooting, was listed in stable condition at a hospital. No officers were injured in the incident.

According to Denver Police Chief Ron Thomas, the sequence began just before 2 a.m. near 16th and California streets, where one person was shot and also hospitalized in stable condition. Officers responding to that initial shooting tracked a suspect moving between California and Stout streets before following him into an apartment vestibule, where they opened fire when he did not comply with commands to disarm.

A multi-agency investigation is now underway, overseen by the Office of the Independent Monitor. Additional details about the circumstances of the first shooting had not been released as of Sunday morning.

stories.headline: Barricade situation in northwest Denver metro ends with officer fatally shooting suspect
stories.summary:
A standoff at a home in the northwest Denver metro area ended in a fatal officer-involved shooting, according to CBS Colorado. Police reported that an officer shot and killed the suspect following the barricade situation, though further details about the location, the identity of those involved, and the events leading to the standoff had not been released.

The incident adds to a series of officer-involved shootings in the Denver metro area in recent days. Authorities did not immediately release additional information about whether the suspect was armed or what prompted the initial law enforcement response.

stories.headline: Person killed in go-kart and vehicle crash in southwest Denver
stories.summary:
One person was killed and another was seriously injured in a late-night collision between a car and a go-kart in southwest Denver on Saturday, according to the Denver Police Department. The crash occurred around 11 p.m. at Evans Avenue and South Federal Boulevard near the Harvey Park neighborhood.

Police declined to specify whether the fatality was the go-kart operator or the car's occupant. A second person was transported to a hospital with serious injuries. The incident was initially mischaracterized on social media as involving a motorcyclist before DPD clarified the other vehicle was a go-kart.

The crash remains under investigation and no arrests or citations had been issued as of Sunday morning.

stories.headline: Community meeting addresses spike in deadly Denver shootings, calls for sustained action
stories.summary:
About 40 residents and government officials gathered Saturday at the Clayton Early Learning campus in Denver for a community meeting organized by the Colorado Black Round Table to address a recent surge in gun violence. Six people were killed in the first 10 days of April alone, prompting calls from community members for more coordinated and sustained prevention efforts.

Denver Police Chief Ron Thomas said investigators do not believe the April homicides were connected but acknowledged a troubling increase in lethality. Thomas said the department has added street cameras and increased patrols while cautioning against over-policing, which he said erodes community trust. Public Safety Executive Director Al Gardner echoed that approach, emphasizing the need for results-driven responses.

State Senate President James Coleman called for investment in Black-owned businesses and nonprofits as a foundation for sustainable funding of violence prevention programs. Community members pressed officials for concrete follow-through on the ideas raised at the meeting, with organizer Helen Bradshaw urging greater collaboration among those already present rather than continued reliance on outside funding.

stories.headline: Avalanche defeat Wild 9-6 in wild Game 1 of Western Conference Second Round despite Makar injury scare
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche opened the Western Conference Second Round with a 9-6 victory over the Minnesota Wild at Ball Arena on Sunday, recovering after blowing an early three-goal lead. Cale Makar scored twice in the third period and added an assist despite leaving the game in the first period after taking a hit from Wild forward Marcus Foligno along the end boards.

Devon Toews contributed a goal and three assists, Nathan MacKinnon added a goal and two assists, and Sam Malinski and Artturi Lehkonen each scored. Scott Wedgewood made 30 saves for Colorado, the No. 1 seed from the Central Division. The Wild received a goal and two assists from Quinn Hughes but could not overcome Colorado's offensive output.

Coach Jared Bednar praised Makar's willingness to return but acknowledged defensive lapses that allowed Minnesota to claw back into contention. Game 2 of the best-of-seven series is scheduled for Tuesday at 8 p.m. ET at Ball Arena.

stories.headline: Nuggets offseason preview: Jokic extension, roster moves loom after first-round playoff exit
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets enter an uncertain offseason following an early first-round playoff exit at the hands of the Minnesota Timberwolves, with a set of high-stakes decisions ahead for ownership and management. At the center of everything is Nikola Jokic, who declined a supermax extension last July with the understanding that a more lucrative deal — estimated at roughly $287 million over four years — would be offered this summer. Jokic has publicly stated his intention to remain in Denver.

The Nuggets face significant salary cap pressure, currently projected as a second-apron team with an estimated $203 million to $213 million committed to roughly 10 players. The team is widely expected to make moves to bring payroll below the second apron threshold, which limits roster-building flexibility. Team options and non-guaranteed salaries will be key levers this offseason.

Beyond Jokic, questions surround the broader roster's ability to contend. Denver has won 50 or more games in four straight regular seasons but has seen its championship ceiling questioned after back-to-back early playoff exits. How general manager Ben Tenzer and the Kroenke family respond this summer could shape the franchise's trajectory for years.

stories.headline: Broncos offseason program begins Monday with Bo Nix sidelined, Burnham stadium plans advance
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos officially began their 2026 offseason program Monday, though quarterback Bo Nix will not be a full participant after recently undergoing a cleanup procedure on his surgically repaired right ankle. Sources indicated the procedure was planned for some point regardless, and the team opted to perform it now rather than risk a mid-season interruption. Nix is expected to be limited through the voluntary and mandatory phases of the offseason, with the priority being his return to full health by training camp in late July.

Head coach Sean Payton delayed the program's start compared to the rest of the NFL, citing the team's deep run in the 2025 season. All of May will consist of weightlifting with no on-field work until June, when the Broncos plan two weeks of OTAs and a minicamp. A rookie minicamp is scheduled for May 8-10.

Separately, the Broncos presented updated plans for their proposed Burnham Yard stadium district to a city advisory committee, showing the most detailed layout yet of the 150-acre mixed-use development. The stadium itself would occupy 30 to 35 acres, with the remainder allocated for parking structures and entertainment zones. Community debate centered on traffic impacts to the adjacent La Alma Lincoln Park neighborhood, particularly a proposed expansion of Eighth Avenue.

stories.headline: Metro Denver condo market offers entry point as insurance costs, HOA fees add risk for buyers
stories.summary:
Denver metro condominiums continue to offer a lower-cost entry into homeownership, with the average multifamily price at $390,000 in March — up 4.5% month-over-month — compared to a median single-family home price of $645,000 and an overall metro median of $590,000. However, real estate professionals are warning buyers to scrutinize rising insurance costs and homeowner association fees before committing to a purchase.

Soaring homeowners insurance premiums — averaging roughly $4,100 annually statewide and significantly higher in wildfire-adjacent areas — have driven up HOA fees for many condo associations, sometimes enough to derail transactions. Brokers say buyers should carefully review association reserves, special assessments, and the ratio of owner-occupied to rented units before going under contract.

The Denver market received additional attention after the S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Index reported Denver has displaced Tampa as the weakest housing market among 20 tracked nationally, with home prices down 2.2% year-over-year through February. National economist Elliott Eisenberg said Denver is nonetheless better positioned than Sunbelt markets further south and east.

stories.headline: Mountain snow and Front Range accumulation possible as strong spring storm targets Colorado this week
stories.summary:
A potentially strong spring storm system is expected to bring moderate to heavy snow to Colorado's mountains and foothills beginning Monday night and continuing through Wednesday, according to the National Weather Service in Boulder. Forecasters say there is a growing chance of several inches of accumulation along the Interstate 25 corridor, though exact totals and the timing of rain-to-snow transitions at lower elevations remained uncertain as of Sunday.

Monday along the Front Range is forecast to reach near 70 degrees before rain begins after noon and temperatures drop sharply by evening. Fort Collins could begin seeing snowfall as early as Monday night. Conditions are expected to clear by Thursday, when a sunny day with a high of 72 degrees is forecast for Denver.

stories.headline: Colorado schools lack standardized drug education, leaving prevention to patchwork of district programs
stories.summary:
Colorado's 'local control' approach to public education means drug prevention curriculum varies dramatically between school districts, with state law requiring only that some form of prevention education be taught without specifying content, according to a Denver Post investigation. Health education specialists say the breadth and depth of coverage ranges widely, leaving many students without consistent or evidence-based information about substances including cannabis and psychedelics, both now legal in Colorado.

The reporting highlights 5280 Recovery High School in Denver — billed as the nation's largest recovery high school — which serves roughly 100 teenagers dealing with substance abuse. The school celebrated an average of 440 days of sobriety among its student body during the 2024-25 academic year, its highest since opening in 2018, using peer accountability, coaching, and open community discussion as core tools.

The story is the second installment in a three-part Denver Post series examining how drug education has evolved as cultural and legal attitudes toward substances shift in Colorado.

stories.headline: Colorado nonprofits and teens fill gaps in school drug education through peer-led programs
stories.summary:
Community-based organizations are playing an increasingly critical role in drug prevention education across Colorado, particularly in rural areas and districts with limited resources, according to the Denver Post. Groups such as Rise Above Colorado and the Western Colorado Area Health Education Center offer youth activities, documentary screenings, naloxone trainings, and peer-driven information campaigns aimed at equipping teenagers with science-based facts about substance use.

Rise Above Colorado's approach focuses on shifting the narrative around teen drug use by highlighting survey data showing that most Colorado youth ages 12 to 17 choose not to use substances. Executive director Kent MacLennan said fostering a sense of belonging and healthy social norms is central to their prevention model, with initiatives such as statewide art contests that connect youth to community projects.

The story concludes a three-part Denver Post series on drug education in Colorado, following earlier installments on how public attitudes have shifted alongside the legalization of cannabis and psychedelics.

stories.headline: Film on the Rocks announces 2026 season at Red Rocks with 'Sinners,' 'The Princess Bride' and 'Jurassic Park'
stories.summary:
Film on the Rocks has announced its 2026 summer season at Red Rocks Amphitheatre, featuring a lineup that includes the recent release 'Sinners' alongside beloved classics 'The Princess Bride' and 'Jurassic Park.' The annual outdoor movie series draws large crowds to the iconic Morrison venue each summer.

The 2026 season adds to a long-running tradition of outdoor cinema at one of Colorado's most recognizable landmarks. Additional screening dates, showtimes, and ticket information were expected to be released ahead of the season.

joke.joke: Sometimes, you have to just turn around in your driveway and wave at your neighbor. That's the whole interaction. It doesn't need to go further.

topStory.headline: Iran Threatens Attack on U.S. Forces in Strait of Hormuz as Washington Launches 'Project Freedom' Escort Mission
topStory.summary:
Iran issued a direct military warning Monday that any American forces entering the Strait of Hormuz would be attacked, as the United States launched a new naval escort operation called Project Freedom to guide commercial vessels through the critical waterway. Iranian Major General Ali Abdollahi warned that foreign armed forces, specifically naming the U.S. military, would face attack if they approached or entered the strait. Iranian state media also claimed two missiles struck a U.S. destroyer near the entrance to the strait, a claim the U.S. military flatly denied.

U.S. Central Command said in a statement on X that no Navy ships had been struck, and a U.S. official separately told NBC News that no American vessels were denied access to the strait on Monday. The command confirmed that Project Freedom successfully guided ships through the strait, with the operation involving 15,000 service members, guided-missile destroyers, more than 100 land- and sea-based aircraft, and unmanned platforms. Iran's Navy also released a map asserting its zone of control over the strait, warning that passage must be coordinated with Tehran.

Separately, the United Arab Emirates confirmed that a tanker affiliated with Adnoc, its state-owned oil company, was struck in the Strait of Hormuz, though no injuries were reported. The UAE's foreign ministry called the attack a flagrant violation of UN Security Council Resolution 2817 and accused Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps of acts of piracy. The UAE demanded Iran immediately cease all hostilities and reopen the strait unconditionally.

President Trump announced Project Freedom in a lengthy Truth Social post Sunday, saying the U.S. would safely escort ships belonging to nations not party to the Middle East conflict out of the strait. The Joint Maritime Information Center advised vessels to route through Omani territorial waters, warning that standard shipping lanes should be considered extremely hazardous due to mines that have not been fully surveyed. The standoff has brought global shipping through the strait to a near standstill and contributed to rising energy prices worldwide.

The Strait of Hormuz carries roughly 20 percent of the world's oil supply, making it one of the most strategically vital maritime chokepoints on earth. The competing claims, active military deployments, and confirmed strike on a commercial tanker signal a significant escalation in tensions between Washington and Tehran, with the potential to further disrupt global energy markets and draw additional regional players into the conflict.
